source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 17 Conclusions - pipes & xargs tips

questions.q: A pipe (|) connects which stream of one command to the next?
options: Only stdout (channel 1); stderr does not go through the pipe by default
questions.why: A pipe carries stdout only; to send stderr through it you must redirect channel 2 onto channel 1 (2>&1).

questions.q: To capture a command's stdout into a file AND merge stderr into it, which is correct?
options: cmd 1>test.txt 2>&1 (redirect stdout first, then point stderr at it)
questions.why: Order matters: send stdout to the file first, THEN 2>&1 makes stderr follow it. 'cmd 2>&1 1>test.txt' does not do what you expect.

questions.q: The tip 'tar uses keys, not options' means what in practice?
options: tar xvf works even where -xvf might not on older systems; and modern tar auto-detects the compression format
tar requires a physical key file
You must always pass a dash
questions.why: tar historically takes bare keys (xvf); you also no longer need to specify z/j on modern tar since it auto-detects compression when reading.

questions.q: Rename every .log file to .log.old, using xargs with the {} placeholder (input from 'ls *.log').
questions.why: -I{} sets a placeholder and runs one command per input line, substituting {} with each filename.

questions.q: For filenames that may contain spaces, which combination is safe with xargs?
options: find ... -print0 paired with xargs -0 (separate on the null byte, not spaces)
questions.why: -print0 / -0 use the null byte as the separator, so spaces in names don't split arguments; -P runs jobs in parallel.
